channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: Garry’s Mod Successor s&box Releases To Mixed Steam Reviews And An Influx Of AI Slop
item.content:encoded:
Also: is L.A. Noir 2 still on the table?

How do you combat user-generated game junk in the age of AI slop? A cool free otter backpack in Final Fantasy 14 turns out to be a big mistake. And today’s big Arc Raiders update accidentally messed up a key feature. It’s your daily Kotaku Checkpoint for April 28, 2026. Everyone is dunking on the price of Valve’s $100 Steam controller, but what about paying $65 for a single button?

The Keychron Q0 Mini 8K Action Key is a single mechanical switch for $65. With endless possibilities, it brings fun and stress relief while boosting your daily productivity. Think of it as the Pomodoro Technique for gamers

Facepunch’s s&box out and off to a rough start

Pitched as a “game creation platform built on the Source 2 engine,” s&box is like Garry’s mod spun into Roblox. “The bones are there, they just need to support singularly ran servers and allow steam workshop support for the game,” reads one player’s assessment. Another was less kind. “s&box – an unfinished mess that doesn’t hold up against modern engines or actual game platforms,” they wrote.

There are also accusations that the game as creation platform is being loaded up with low-effort AI slop. Facepunch founder Garry Newman said the team is aware of various complaints and working on improvements, including good player-created content pushing generative AI junk out of view.

“Low quality, obvious AI-created slop is going to be a growing problem in every creative outlet,” he told Rock, Paper, Shotgun. “We don’t encourage using AI to be creative. We don’t encourage using AI to create games for you. But we do acknowledge that it’s a good learning tool and it’s a good productivity tool. We’ll be taking action to promote human creativity and push obviously AI-created slop off the main page.”

Former Witcher 3 director thinks generative AI can be useful

“I think that companies should use AI, but in the way which helps people to work, not replace the people,” he said in a recent Q&A for his next game, The Blood of Dawnwalker, according to IGN. Rebel Wolves, the studio behind the upcoming RPG, used AI for voicing scenes during the script writing phase of development but later removed them A spokesperson was quick to state unequivocally that there is no AI in the finished version of the game.

“I want to make one thing absolutely clear: nothing that’s in The Blood of Dawnwalker was created using generative AI,” they reportedly said. “Nothing. People with blood and flesh made this game from the beginning to the end.”

Yes, Resident Evil‘s Leon was named after THAT Leon

RE2 director Hideki Kamiya confirmed the horror thirst-trap’s name-sake 28 years later. It does indeed come from Léon: The Professional starring Jean Reno. “I’m surprised by all the reactions to Leon’s naming… Didn’t I mention it…? It’s not something to hide, and I think I would’ve answered if asked,” Kamiya wrote on X. “Back during development, I consulted with Mikami-san about naming the protagonist, and after getting shot down on a few ideas, I suggested, “How about we take it from that movie I watched the other day?” and proposed “Leon,” to which he said, “That works, doesn’t it?”

Jurassic Park star Sam Neill is cancer free and ready for more movies

“I’ve had a scan just now, and there is no cancer in my body. This is an extraordinary thing,” Neil told 7News. “Science at its best.” The recovery was due to a new cutting-edge  CAR T-cell therapy treatment. He said he’s ready to start starring in new movies again which I’m all onboard with as long as they don’t de-age him again. in the meantime you can watch him tell you all about his beautiful plum harvest from last summer.

Otter backpacks have come to FF14 and no one knows why

Fans are confused but enjoying the neat surprise. Unfortunately, it’s from a bug. “Upon investigating this issue, we confirmed that during data configuration, the internal ID for the ‘Senor Otter pack’ was mistakenly set to the same internal ID as the ‘Black Embroidered Parasol,'” reads a new notice on the MMO’s forum page (via PC Gamer). “As a result, if you have already obtained the ‘Black Embroidered Parasol,’ you will also be able to use ‘Senior Otter pack.'”

Mobile spin-off The Division Resurgence is now on PC, too

The free-to-play RPG looter shooter came to iOS and Android already and now has a technical test on PC ahead of its full launch in August during season 2.

Arc Raiders‘ newest update breaks crossplay

The developers are aware and working on a hotfix.

Will we ever get L.A. Noir 2?

Maybe, according to Take-Two CEO Strauss Zelnick. “You never know,” he said at this week’s iicon in Las Vegas, Game File reports. “he answer broadly is we’re looking at doing something in the future with all of our intellectual property, but nothing to announce.”

ICYMI:

  • The Call of Duty movie director used to think games were a waste of time (and maybe still does?)
  • The best SNES emulator around is getting a complete overhaul
  • Valve’s new Steam controller is almost here: read our review

Watch this:



https://kotaku.com/garrys-mod-sbox-steam-reviews-facepunch-ai-2000691298
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Hackers Have Bypassed Denuvo DRM In Every Game And Now 2K Is Reportedly Fighting Back With 14-Day Online Check-Ins
item.content:encoded:
Using a relatively new bypass technique, hackers claim to have effectively cracked all games using Denuvo protection software

The much-hated and controversial video game DRM tech Denuvo has seemingly been completely and utterly cracked thanks to a new technique created by crafty pirates and modders. And now that the once impossible-to-defeat DRM has been bypassed, Borderlands publisher 2K is fighting back with Denuvo’s help and reportedly implementing new 14-day online check-ins for games on PC.

As recently reported by Tom’s Hardware, on April 27, a large Reddit thread tracking which games using Denvuo DRM still needed to be cracked or bypassed officially hit zero. (This list tracks games that don’t require an online server connection, not MMORPGs and other games that do.)  What that means, effectively, is that according to Denuvo modders and hackers, the DRM tech is no longer able to stop pirates from downloading and installing games for free. This milestone for hackers is largely thanks to the MKDev collective and modder DenuvOwO. It was these people who created the hypervisor-based bypass (HVB) that installs a kernel-level driver to bypass Denuvo’s DRM checks.

Technically, Denuvo is still in the game, but it isn’t functioning as it should, and pirates can play without paying. And there is already some evidence that bypassing Denuvo has led to performance improvements in titles like Resident Evil Requiem, which might push some people to use the bypass even if they bought the game legally. We saw this in a previous Resident Evil game when hackers bypassed Denuvo in 2021.

While it’s debatable whether DRM circumvention is completely bad, as video game preservation relies heavily on cracked games to keep them playable long after the publisher moves on or delists a title, there’s no question that if you make, publish, and sell video games, this isn’t the news you want to hear about the DRM software you rely on to stop piracy. So, expectedly, some publishers as well as Denuvo itself are reportedly fighting back.

As reported by Pirat Nation and Tom’s Hardware, 2K Games has apparently begun adding 14-day online check-ins to some of its PC games. The check-in has apparently been added to NBA 2K25, NBA 2K26, and Marvel’s Midnight Suns. These games now reportedly use a “fixed offline authorization token” that expires after two weeks. Once that happens, the game will not be playable until you connect to the internet and let the game ping Denvuo to get a new token. Pirat Nation and hackers are claiming this new countdown isn’t properly disclosed on the games’ Steam Store page or in each title’s respective EULA.

Kotaku has reached out to 2K and Denuvo, but didn’t hear back before publication.



https://kotaku.com/hackers-have-bypassed-denuvo-drm-in-every-game-and-now-2k-is-reportedly-fighting-back-with-14-day-online-check-ins-2000691311
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
